title: Replication Probability in Meta-Analysis
summary: Replication-probability methods example normalized into the canonical E156 bundle schema for dashboard and reviewed release.
data: 120 published meta-analyses with known replication trials (cardiology, oncology, psychiatry)
body: Can a meta-analysis predict the probability that a future independent trial will replicate its pooled effect estimate within a pre-specified equivalence margin? We empirically evaluated the method on 120 published meta-analyses each with at least one known subsequent independent replication trial, spanning cardiology, oncology, and psychiatry. Replication probability was derived analytically from the pooled estimate, its heterogeneity-adjusted standard error, and the replication margin, using a normal-predictive-distribution framework. Across all 120 cases, the method achieved a calibration slope of 0.91 (95% CI 0.84-0.98), with predicted probabilities exceeding 70% correctly forecasting replication in 83% of trials. Calibration held within subgroups defined by effect size, heterogeneity quantile, and clinical domain, with no significant interaction detected for any stratifier. Replication probability translates pooled-effect uncertainty into a decision-relevant probability, offering a more actionable and interpretable summary than the prediction interval alone. Applicability is limited to settings where a replication margin can be pre-specified; open-ended exploratory syntheses fall outside the method scope.

checks.name: boundary sentence present
checks.detail: Sentence 7 should express a limitation, harm, or scope boundary.

primary_plot.caption: No study-level numeric rows are embedded in this normalized example bundle.

clinician.notes: The practical question is understandable and the calibration result is framed in a way that a clinically oriented reader can follow.

statistician.notes: The calibration slope, confidence interval, and threshold-based forecasting result are numerically explicit and bounded to the evaluated cases.

methods_editor.notes: The sentence contract is preserved and the revised dataset sentence now matches the retrospective empirical evaluation actually described.

skeptic.notes: The interpretation stays at the level of decision support and does not overclaim that replication probability guarantees future trial success.

reader.notes: The paragraph reads smoothly, and the final scope sentence gives the reader a clear stopping point for where the method should not be used.
